One of those libraries -**`textual`**- is what we'll talk about now.

Looking at the textual GitHub repository, it looks like it was created around 2 years ago by Will McGugan, who also created the well-known `rich` Python library. In fact, Textual is built on top of `rich` with an overall aim of allowing developers to,

> "Build sophisticated user interfaces with a simple Python API ... that can be run in the terminal or a browser"

For the rest of this article, I'll take you through some coding exercises so you can see textual in use. I'll leave a link to the textual GitHub repository at the end.

Textual runs in Windows, macOS or Linux and requires Python 3.8 or higher.

I'll be using WSL2 Ubuntu Linux for my code development. If you're a Windows user, I have a comprehensive guide on installing WSL2, which you can find [here](https://medium.com/stackademic/installing-wsl2-ubuntu-for-windows-81122c551bc2).

### Craft good

looking terminal applications

> This is the third in my series of useful but less well-known Python libraries. You can read the other two by clicking on **[more_itertools](https://medium.com/@thomas_reid/introducing-the-more-itertools-python-library-e5a24a901979)** and **[plumbum](https://medium.com/gitconnected/useful-python-libraries-you-might-not-know-existed-plumbum-01be50863bd3)**

In this day of HTML, CSS, etc., is there room for terminal applications that interact with users?

My answer is a resounding yes - especially if they look half-decent.

Moreover, a few Python libraries are now available that can abstract away many of the complications around creating terminal apps, which is a win-win situation.

One of those libraries -**`textual`**- is what we'll talk about now.

Here’s a concise summary about the `textual` and `rich` Python libraries, using DeepLake-RAG for up-to-date, practical information:

- **`rich`** is a popular Python library for creating beautiful, formatted console output. It supports color, tables, markdown, syntax highlighting, progress bars, and more, making terminal output much more readable and visually appealing.

- **`textual`** is built on top of `rich` and allows you to create sophisticated, interactive user interfaces directly in the terminal (and potentially in the browser). It provides a simple Python API for building terminal apps with layouts, widgets, and event handling, abstracting away much of the complexity of TUI (Text User Interface) development.

**Key Points:**
- Both libraries are actively maintained and require Python 3.8+.
- `textual` is ideal for building modern, interactive terminal applications, while `rich` is perfect for enhancing the look and feel of your console output.
- They are cross-platform (Windows, macOS, Linux).

**Resources:**
- [Textual GitHub](https://github.com/Textualize/textual)
- [Rich GitHub](https://github.com/Textualize/rich)
- [Textual tutorial article](https://levelup.gitconnected.com/useful-python-libraries-you-might-not-know-existed-textual-810635ecff79)
